data.title: Pnc expects 8% loan growth and 11% revenue increase in 2026 while integrating FirstBank
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: The PNC Financial Services Group, Inc. (PNC) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO William Demchak highlighted, "we earned $7 billion in net income or $16.59 per share. Strong performance across all our lines of business resulted in record revenue, 5% operating leverage and 21% EPS growth for the year." He announced the closing of the FirstBank acquisition on January 5 and noted, "we are poised to accelerate that growth in 2026," with expectations for FirstBank to add approximately $1 per share to 2027 results. Demchak emphasized ongoing investments: "one of the largest investment agendas we've ever pursued, including all of our technology initiatives, payments capabilities and consumer rewards platforms and of course, our branch expansions."

  • CFO Robert Reilly reported, "loans of $328 billion grew by $2 billion or 1%" and "deposit balances were up $8 billion or 2% and averaged $440 billion." He stated, "our tangible book value of $112.51 per common share increased 4% linked quarter and 18% compared to the same period a year ago." Reilly added, "we returned $1.1 billion of capital to shareholders," and outlined plans to "increase our quarterly share repurchases to a range of $600 million to $700 million."

Outlook

  • Reilly provided full-year 2026 outlook: "we expect full year average loan growth to be approximately 8%. We expect total revenue to be up approximately 11%. Inside of that, our expectation is for net interest income to be up approximately 14%, and noninterest income to grow 6%. Noninterest expense to be up approximately 7% excluding an estimated $325 million of integration expense." He also projected an effective tax rate of approximately 19.5% and "400 basis points of positive operating leverage, nearly all of which is driven by PNC on a stand-alone basis."

  • For Q1 2026, guidance includes "average loans to be up approximately 5%. Net interest income to be up approximately 6%. Fee income to be down 1% to 2%. Other noninterest income to be in the range of $150 million to $200 million. Taking the component pieces of revenue together, we expect total revenue to be up 2% to 3%."

Financial Results

  • Reilly stated, "total revenue was a record $6.1 billion and grew $156 million or 3%. Noninterest expense of $3.6 billion increased $142 million or 4%. And as a result, we delivered record PPNR of $2.5 billion." Fourth quarter net income was reported as $2 billion or $4.88 per diluted share. Net interest income was $3.7 billion, up $83 million or 2%, and net interest margin was 2.84%. Noninterest income of $2.3 billion increased $73 million or 3%.

  • Asset management and brokerage increased $7 million or 2%. Capital markets and advisory revenue increased $57 million or 13% driven by M&A advisory revenue. Mortgage revenue decreased $13 million or 8%. The Visa derivative adjustment in the fourth quarter was negative $41 million.

  • Credit quality remained strong with nonperforming loans representing 0.67% of total loans at year-end, total delinquencies at 0.44%, and net loan charge-offs at $162 million. Allowance for credit losses was $5.2 billion or 1.58% of total loans.

Q&A

  • John Pancari, Evercore ISI: Asked about the sustainability of increased share repurchases, to which Reilly responded, "$600 million to $700 million is a quarterly pace that we expect to continue through '26."

  • Pancari followed up on CET1 targets; Reilly said, "we've said 10%. So in the first quarter, we'll be in that 10.2%, 10.3%, working our way down from 10.6%."

  • Robert Siefers, Piper Sandler: Inquired about NII momentum; Reilly responded, "guidance with FirstBank for the year...is up 14% in NII. Inside of that...PNC stand-alone, we're somewhere between 7.5% and 8%."

  • Betsy Graseck, Morgan Stanley: Sought details on technology investment. Demchak explained, "we maybe spend $3.5 billion and it's going to go up 10% plus or minus for the year. And inside of that, AI is 20% of that increase."

  • Steven Chubak, Wolfe Research: Asked about NIM trajectory; Reilly stated, "our plans in '26 are to reach that 3% level in the second half of '26, somewhere during the third quarter, maybe the end of the third quarter."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on sustainability of capital returns, NII momentum, and the integration of FirstBank, pressing for clarity on guidance and strategic investments. The tone was neutral, with some positive acknowledgment of operating leverage and capital management.

  • Management maintained a confident tone in both prepared remarks and Q&A, emphasizing strong growth, capital flexibility, and investment discipline. Responses were direct and assertive, as seen in Demchak’s and Reilly’s explanations regarding capital strategy and technology investment priorities.

  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s tone remained confident, but with increased emphasis on integration of FirstBank and future growth targets. Analyst tone was similarly persistent, with recurring questions on growth sustainability and capital allocation.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance shifted from stable-to-modest growth in Q3 to more robust targets in Q4, specifically with full-year 2026 expectations for 8% loan growth and 11% revenue growth, largely attributed to the FirstBank acquisition and organic expansion.

  • Strategic focus expanded from organic growth and ongoing branch builds to include a major acquisition and aggressive investment in technology and automation.

  • Analyst questions in both quarters centered on NII, capital returns, and deposit dynamics, but Q4 featured more follow-up on integration of FirstBank and technology spend.

  • Key metrics saw higher loan and deposit balances, record revenue, and improved tangible book value in Q4. Management’s confidence increased, highlighting large-scale investment and integration efforts.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited integration risks with FirstBank, noting "nonrecurring merger and integration costs is approximately $325 million, the majority of which will be recognized in the first half of 2026."

  • Reilly stated, "CRE balances have largely stabilized, and we anticipate moderate growth in 2026," but acknowledged potential headwinds in residential real estate loans.

  • Economic risks were mainly characterized as external/exogenous, with Demchak saying, "It has to be some exogenous variable because the base economy, I just don't see big cracks that are going to be realized in '26."

Final Takeaway

PNC capped a strong year with record financial results and the transformational acquisition of FirstBank, setting the stage for accelerated growth in 2026. Management projects significant increases in loans, revenues, and operating leverage, underpinned by disciplined capital returns and continued investments in technology and branch expansion. The company is committed to integrating FirstBank efficiently while targeting substantial operating and revenue synergies, supporting strong forward momentum into the coming year.

data.title: Honeywell upgraded at J.P. Morgan on 'disconnect' to asset value; 3M downgraded
data.description:
Honeywell (HON) +2.1% in Friday's trading as J.P. Morgan upgraded shares to Overweight from Neutral with a $255 price target, raised from $218, saying the stock has extended its discount to a sum-of-the-parts valuation, comfortably off its highs compared to SOTP peers that are up a weighted 20%.

Honeywell's (HON) "messy 2026 EPS profile, which will be cleared with guide, as creating an opportunity ahead of the looming break-up, on which we should get clarity on many key fronts in the next six months," JPM analyst Stephen Tusa said.

Aerospace remains the key driver, as Honeywell (HON) expects all markets to grow by high single digits this year, but Tusa sees potential upside as industry participants expect another year of double-digit commercial OE and aftermarket growth, and optimism around the business jet demand environment likely will continue, where the company has outsized exposure in the super mid-size and above, which is the high value portion of the portfolio; additionally, rising geopolitical tensions and the prospect for FY 2027 budget growth have improved the outlook for defense.

Meanwhile, Tusa downgraded 3M (MMM) cut to Neutral from Overweight with a $182 PT, citing below-sector near-term earnings growth potential after having materially outperformed since new management came in, while applauding execution on cultural and operational improvements beyond expectations.

"While there is clearly momentum, we believe the early innings low-hanging fruit is behind in terms of the self help initiatives, solidly transitioning into the next phase which is reinvigorating the innovation engine, which we think will take time," Tusa wrote.

data.title: RTX extends rally for seventh straight session
data.description:
Shares of RTX Corporation (RTX) rose 0.96% to $201.75 in the afternoon trade on Friday, extending gains for a seventh consecutive session.

The stock had gained 6.76% between January 8 and January 15, significantly outperforming the S&P 500, which rose 0.33% over the same period.

RTX has been in focus amid a series of developments tied to the U.S. aerospace and defense sector, as investors look ahead to the upcoming earnings season against a backdrop of renewed expectations for higher defense spending. Sentiment has been shaped by commentary pointing to a strong demand environment, alongside headlines linked to former President Donald Trump’s remarks on missile defense initiatives and proposals for a sharply higher U.S. defense budget.

At the same time, some analysts have cautioned that the recent rally across aerospace and defense stocks could raise expectations heading into earnings, even as the sector continues to outperform the broader market.

According to Seeking Alpha’s Quant rating system, RTX is rated Hold, with a score of 3.47 out of 5, with grades of A+ in profitability and revisions, an A- in momentum, but partly offset by weaker grades of C in growth and D- in valuation.

Meanwhile, Seeking Alpha analysts are broadly bullish on the stock.

Turning to Wall Street, 13 out of 22 analysts covering RTX rate the stock a buy or higher, seven recommend holding, and two suggest sell or lower.

An analyst reiterated a cautious view on RTX, saying the stock “remains a ‘Hold’ as shares approach fair value after a strong run and premium valuation,” despite solid operating performance. The analyst highlighted “13% organic sales growth” and “$4 billion free cash flow” in the latest quarter, but cautioned that RTX trades “near 30x forward earnings and 3x sales,” leaving limited room for upside.

Shares of RTX have gained 12.57% over the past month and are up around 9% year to date.

data.title: U.S. stocks seesaw, even as some chipmakers advance
data.description:
Wall Street on Friday searched for direction, with losses in materials and utilities stocks offset by gains in some chipmakers and the industrial sector. The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) and the tech-heavy Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) struggled for momentum.

The Nasdaq (COMP:IND) was essentially flat in midday trade, while the S&P (SP500) edged higher by +0.06%. The blue-chip Dow Jones Industrial Average (DJI) slipped -0.09%.  

Chipmakers extended their gains from the previous session in which they were buoyed by strong results from Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM) and bargain hunting. Micron (MU) +5.79%, Lam Research (LRCX) +2.67%, and AMD (AMD) +2.58% were among the top percentage gainers on the Nasdaq (COMP:IND).

Over in the bond market, Treasury yields rose across the curve. The U.S. 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) climbed to 3.60%, while the benchmark U.S. 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) advanced to 4.21%. The longer-dated U.S. 30-year Treasury yield (US30Y) increased to 4.82%.

Top S&P 500 gainers included Super Micro Computer (SMCI) +9.06%, Qnity Electronics (Q) +7.43%, and GE Vernova (GEV) +6.30%. Decliners included Constellation Energy (CEG) -9.67%, Amcor (AMCR) -6.54%, and Albemarle (ALB) -6.20%.

data.title: SA analyst upgrades/downgrades: ZS, LRCX, NXPI, RKLB
data.description:
Recent analyst actions include Bay Area Ideas upgrading Zscaler (ZS) to Hold, citing improving AI monetization and a more reasonable valuation. Meanwhile, Dhierin Bechai has upgraded Lam Research (LRCX) to Buy, driven by robust wafer fabrication equipment demand and memory supply bottlenecks. On the downside, Brett Ashcroft Green maintains a Hold rating on NXP Semiconductors (NXPI), noting that 2026 growth appears already priced in. Dhierin Bechai has also downgraded Rocket Lab (RKLB) to Sell, warning that Neutron rocket euphoria has pushed the stock far ahead of its fundamentals.

Upgrades

  • Zscaler (ZS): Upgrade Sell to Hold by Bay Area Ideas. The analyst cites Q1 results showing AI innovation translating into financial growth, combined with a more reasonable valuation following a 27% stock decline since the previous downgrade.

    “Q1 results indicate that AI innovation is now starting to translate into financial growth. FY2026 guidance is mixed, but still, there are signs the long-term trajectory of the business is improving. … While the valuation still isn’t cheap, I would say that the forward P/E now offers a much fairer reflection of where the fundamentals are at.”

  • Lam Research (LRCX): Upgrade Hold to Buy by Dhierin Bechai. The analyst points to revised wafer fab spending forecasts calling for 10–15% YoY growth, supporting higher sales and margin expansion for the semiconductor equipment maker.

    “I am currently applying a valuation that prices the stock at three times the median given strength in wafer fabrication equipment demand. As a result, I am rating the stock a buy with a $246.84 base case price target, indicating 13% upside and 33% upside in a more bullish setting with a $291.11 price target.”

Downgrades

  • NXP Semiconductors (NXPI): Hold maintained by Brett Ashcroft Green. Despite healthy 2026 earnings growth expectations, the analyst believes current pricing leaves limited margin of safety, with automotive semiconductor commoditization remaining a key risk.

    “Without providing new and interesting integrations, I feel the automotive and handset IoT segment of semiconductors risks becoming more and more commoditized. … On the positive side, 2026 looks to be a healthy growth year ahead. That growth, in my opinion, is already priced in where the stock sits unless there is an automotive semiconductor multiple re-rate.”

  • Rocket Lab (RKLB): Downgrade Buy to Sell by Dhierin Bechai. The analyst warns that excessive valuation driven by Neutron rocket euphoria is disconnected from near-term financial prospects, with sales-based valuation methods indicating 20–38% downside risk.

    “While I do believe that Rocket Lab could continue seeing higher share price on extremely bullish sentiment, I do believe that the current share prices are unhealthy, as there is no reasonable foundation for the current stock prices when looking at the expected financial performance in the years ahead. … The share price has been a nice way for investors to make money, but in that process the touch with the fundamentals has completely vanished.”

data.title: Novartis wins FDA breakthrough status for Sjögren’s disease therapy
data.description:
Novartis (NVS) announced on Friday that the U.S. Food and Drug Administration ((FDA)) granted its Breakthrough Therapy designation for ianalumab, its experimental therapy for a multi-organ autoimmune condition known as Sjögren’s disease.

Concurrently, the Swiss drugmaker said that beginning in early 2026 it will start global regulatory submissions for ianalumab, which, if approved, could be the first targeted treatment for this patient group.

The FDA's Breakthrough Therapy designation aims to speed up the development and review of treatments targeted at serious or life-threatening conditions.

Sjögren’s disease is the second most common rheumatic autoimmune disease, and there are no effective treatments for the condition, which affects roughly 0.25% of the population, according to the company.

With the full features of the FDA breakthrough designation, a drug developer can benefit from extensive regulatory guidance with potential eligibility for the FDA’s priority review.

Ianalumab has shown efficacy against Sjögren’s disease, as evident from Novartis’ (NVS) NEPTUNUS-1 and NEPTUNUS-2 Phase 3 trials, from which topline data were shared by the company in August.

data.title: Why investors should be more cautious about the markets
data.description:
Adam Parker, Trivariate Research founder and CEO, said he feels cautious about the stock market.

In an interview with CNBC, the strategist expressed growing caution about the current environment, a notable shift from the previous three years. The primary concern centers on elevated expectations, with the analyst noting that “the penalty for missing estimates is a lot harsher than the reward for beating estimates” in today’s market.

To navigate these risks, Parker recommended an overweight position in healthcare stocks (XLV), (IYH). He emphasized that the sector provides crucial diversification in a portfolio dominated by technology themes. Despite the positioning being “wrong for the first six months,” recent performance has improved, and he said he remains committed to the strategy.

Parker described the S&P 500 (SP500) as “kind of two-thirds an AI ETF.” For the AI trade to continue working, capital expenditure needs to hit a “Goldilocks” level—rising enough to validate returns but not so much that it raises concerns.

He pointed to TSMC’s (TSM) recent results as encouraging, noting that Taiwan Semiconductor is “considered pretty sober” and likely “the most important asset on Earth.”

Earnings expectations for the year remain ambitious, with tech sector (XLK), (IYW) growth projected at 30% and overall growth at 15%. Parker warned that much of this growth is “back half loaded,” suggesting that while January and April should be manageable, “the pressure mounts as we get into the second half of the year.”

Adding to the complexity is uncertainty around Federal Reserve policy. He admitted to being “a little confused about how to interpret [the Fed],” questioning whether future rate cuts would signal economic strength or indicate that “things get bad.” The one exception: balance sheet expansion remains a reliable tailwind for markets.

Lastly, he highlighted an unexpected correlation, observing that gold (XAUUSD:CUR) now appears linked to the AI trade.

“I wouldn’t have said that two years ago,” Parker noted, suggesting that traditional diversification assumptions may no longer hold.

data.title: Trump threatens new tariffs on nations opposing U.S. control of Greenland
data.description:
U.S. President Donald Trump on Friday said he may impose new tariffs on countries that resist his efforts to take control of Greenland.

“I may put a tariff on countries if they don’t go along with Greenland, because we need Greenland for national security,” Trump said at a White House healthcare event. He didn't provide any additional details.

Trump and his team have been considering options for acquiring Greenland, and they have not ruled out the use of the U.S. military as an option, although diplomacy is the preferred route. Officials of Greenland and Denmark have unequivocally stated that the island is not for sale.

Meanwhile, a group of U.S. senators and representatives held a meeting with members of Denmark's parliament in Copenhagen on Friday, Bloomberg reported, with protests set for Saturday opposing Trump's plans.

The gathering was said to have followed days of talks in Washington by Danish Foreign Minister Lars Lokke Rasmussen, who met with U.S. lawmakers after holding talks with Vice President JD Vance and Secretary of State Marco Rubio, alongside Greenland's foreign minister, Vivian Motzfeldt. 

Danish and Greenlandic officials are lobbying Congress for backing what they believe could deter any future pressure from the White House over Greenland, given Congress's control over government spending.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Netflix Q4 Preview: Warner Bros. deal to take spotlight
data.description:
Netflix’s (NFLX) fourth-quarter earnings report on Tuesday is likely to get overshadowed by the Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD) deal, amid the streaming giant’s heated battle with Paramount to take control of one of the ‌iconic studios in Hollywood.

Netflix, in December, first announced plans to acquire Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD) for an enterprise value of about $82.7 billion. Since then, the company has faced opposition from rival bidder Paramount Skydance (PSKY), who has repeatedly argued that its all-cash bid for Warner Bros. is superior to Netflix's cash-and-stock deal.

Netflix, which is reportedly expected to change to an all-cash bid for Warner Bros., has lost 12% since it announced its plan to acquire Warner Bros. Overall, the stock has gained nearly 6% last year, compared to the near 17% rise in the broader S&P 500 Index.

Seeking Alpha analyst Dilantha De Silva noted that even though the company is well-positioned for long-term growth, offering an all-cash deal for Warner Bros. will be a drag on Netflix's projected 2026 earnings per share.

Coming to earnings, analysts believe steady subscriber growth, along with accelerated ad revenue is expected to positively impact Netflix’s results and to lift the stock higher, especially after a weak third quarter report.

Wall Street expects California-based Netflix to post a nearly 17% rise in revenue during the fourth-quarter, thanks to a slew of content including the final season of the popular sci-fi series Stranger Things, the Christmas NFL games as well as the third instalment of the "Knives Out" movie featuring Daniel Craig.

Netflix is expected to post fourth-quarter EPS of $0.55 on revenue of $11.97 billion.

“With much still to prove, we think Netflix is positioning for substantial growth in global advertising, and that should not be overlooked,” said Wedbush analyst Alicia Reese, adding that the firm expects ad revenue to “become Netflix’s primary revenue driver in 2026, with significant opportunities in 2027.”

Seeking Alpha analysts and Seeking Alpha’s Quant ratings consider the stock a Hold, while Wall Street analysts are bullish and rated it a Buy.

Over the last three months, EPS estimates have seen 18 upward revisions, compared to nine downward revisions, while revenue estimates have been revised upwards 25 times versus six downward moves.

data.title: WaFd outlines 8–12% active loan portfolio growth target while margin pressure persists
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: WaFd, Inc. (WAFD) Q1 2026

Management View

  • CEO Brent Beardall emphasized a continued strategic realignment to focus on business banking, stating the Build 2030 plan aims to grow noninterest-bearing deposits to 20% of total deposits by 2030, from 12.6% currently: "The most important goal is increasing our noninterest-bearing deposits to total deposits from 11% last year, up to 20% by 2030, and we are currently sitting at 12.6% today."
  • Beardall highlighted the shift in organizational structure, noting the reorganization of frontline bankers and the achievement of preferred SBA lender status: "It was just January last year that we recognized or reorganized our frontline bankers into 3 segments -- 3 teams to kick off Build 2030. During that time, we've become a preferred SBA lender and 98% of our branch managers...have now passed our small business credit certification process."
  • CFO Kelli Holz reported net income available to common shareholders of $60.5 million, or $0.79 per diluted share, for the quarter ended December 31, 2025, compared to $0.72 per share for the September '25 quarter. She stated, "The $0.07 increase in earnings per share for the quarter was a result of improvements in both income and expense, a modest increase in net interest income and increased noninterest income as well as an overall decrease in total noninterest expense."
  • Holz disclosed active loan originations of $1.1 billion, with repayments and payoffs at $1 billion, and a $240 million decrease in loans receivable, primarily in inactive loan categories. She also noted, "Total investments and mortgage-backed securities increased $728 million during the quarter, funded primarily by the increase in borrowings of $671 million."
  • Beardall discussed aggressive share repurchases: "We repurchased 2 million shares at a price of $29.75 or 99% of tangible book value. Over the last 7 quarters, your company has repurchased 5.8 million shares at a weighted price of $29.45."
  • Chief Credit Officer Ryan Mauer addressed credit quality, citing $1.1 billion new loan production in active portfolios and a decline in adversely classified loans, but an increase in criticized loans to 4.6% of net loans.

Outlook

  • Beardall reiterated the goal for noninterest-bearing deposits to reach 20% of total deposits by 2030 as part of Build 2030. He also projected active loan portfolio growth of 8% to 12% over the next 1 to 2 years, stating, "We believe that we have the products and teams in place to grow our active loan portfolios by 8% to 12% over the next 1 to 2 years."
  • Beardall signaled optimism for margin improvement: "If we can get our margin up to 3% which is our short-term goal within the next 2 years."
  • Lending pipelines are expanding, with Beardall reporting, "Our lending pipeline is up $697 million or 28% over the last quarter."
  • Holz cautioned on near-term margin pressure due to additional mortgage-backed securities purchases, but expects increased net interest income: "Looking forward, I would expect more pressure on the margin from additional mortgage-backed securities purchases in addition to increased net interest income."

Financial Results

  • Holz reported net interest income increased $1.2 million from the prior quarter, with a net interest margin of 2.7% in the December quarter compared to 2.71% for the September quarter.
  • Noninterest income rose $1.9 million to $20.3 million, including a $3.2 million gain on sale of a branch property.
  • Noninterest expense decreased $1.3 million, improving the efficiency ratio to 55.3% from 56.8% in the prior quarter.
  • 1.95 million shares of common stock were repurchased at a weighted average price of $29.75.
  • Core deposits ended the quarter at 79.7% of total deposits; noninterest-bearing deposits were 12.6% of total deposits.
  • Nonperforming assets increased to $203 million or 0.75% of total assets, with delinquent loans rising to 1.07% of total loans.

Q&A

  • Matthew Clark, Piper Sandler, asked about the margin outlook and plans for $800 million in borrowings due: Beardall responded, "Simple, we will replace that with current borrowers not looking to shrink at this point. So we'll replace it, and if the Fed continues to cut rates, that rate will come down."
  • Clark inquired about nonaccrual interest reversal: Holz answered, "Certainly, for the quarter, nonaccrual interest amounted to just over $5 million."
  • Clark asked about the two new C&I nonaccruals: Mauer responded, "One is in manufacturing business being impacted by markets tariff situations, cost of labor, those sorts of things. The other business is a real estate-related entity -- commercial real estate."
  • Clark questioned expense growth: Beardall stated, "We'll have our annual merit increases...but no significant plans for increases of large teams coming over."
  • Jeff Rulis, D.A. Davidson, asked about margin pressure and NII growth: Holz answered, "With the current strategy to replace single-family runoff with mortgage-backed securities."
  • Rulis inquired about loan portfolio runoff: Beardall said, "The inactive could spike up for us if we have a reduction in long-term rates..."
  • Rulis asked about buyback sensitivity: Beardall noted, "The closer we are to tangible book value, the more aggressive we'd be."
  • Andrew Terrell, Stephens, asked about margin reset expectations: Beardall clarified, "I think we're referring to the spot rate, not the reported amount."
  • Terrell inquired about securities mix targets: Beardall responded, "Over the longer term, 25% to 30% wouldn't be out of the question."
  • Terrell asked about transactional deposit growth: Beardall attributed growth to business shift and seasonality.
  • Kelly Motta, KBW, asked about MBS purchases and borrowings: Holz confirmed acceleration of purchases, stating future growth may use borrowings and deposit growth.
  • Motta asked about active portfolio growth timing: Beardall projected 6% to 10% growth in fiscal 2026 and higher in 2027, subject to pipeline pull-through.
  • Motta questioned CRA rating impact: Beardall stated, "Having a Needs to Improve, doesn't preclude you from doing a merger and acquisition, it just makes it much more difficult."
  • Motta inquired about 3% margin trajectory: Beardall said, "We're kind of baking in 1 to 2 cuts this year into that assumption."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts raised concerns about margin outlook, loan portfolio runoff, expense management, and credit quality, with a neutral to slightly negative tone, particularly on margin pressure and asset quality.
  • Management maintained a confident and optimistic tone during prepared remarks, emphasizing strategic progress and improved operational metrics. During Q&A, management responses remained measured, occasionally cautious, especially regarding margin outlook and credit risks.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone was more confident on growth initiatives but more guarded regarding margin and asset quality, while analysts maintained a similar level of scrutiny.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for active loan portfolio growth remains consistent at 8% to 12%, but the projected realization has shifted, with Beardall now outlining 6% to 10% growth for 2026 and higher for 2027.
  • Margin pressure has become more prominent, with management noting the impact of increased nonaccruals and mortgage-backed securities purchases; last quarter, there was cautious optimism for modest margin expansion.
  • Noninterest expense improved this quarter, with a lower efficiency ratio, while noninterest income increased due to gains on asset sales.
  • Credit quality metrics have deteriorated, with higher nonperforming assets and delinquency rates, partly attributed to a few large relationships.
  • Analysts remained focused on margin, credit quality, loan growth, and operational efficiency, with recurring questions on margin trajectory and credit risks.
  • Management's confidence in achieving strategic goals has increased, but caution is evident regarding near-term margin and credit trends.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management identified persistent headwinds from elevated interest rates and economic uncertainty, including the impact of tariffs on borrower financial health.
  • Increased criticized and nonperforming loans were largely driven by two commercial relationships, with active monitoring and engagement to resolve issues.
  • Margin pressure is expected from further mortgage-backed securities purchases, though management anticipates associated income growth.
  • The CRA "Needs to Improve" rating continues to present operational and strategic challenges, particularly for branch relocations and potential M&A.
  • Deposit growth remains challenging, despite recent improvements in transaction accounts.

Final Takeaway

WaFd's first quarter of fiscal 2026 marked notable progress in strategic execution, with active loan originations outpacing repayments, improved earnings per share, and a continued pivot towards business banking under the Build 2030 plan. Management reiterated its goals for loan and deposit growth, while also acknowledging near-term headwinds from margin pressure, heightened credit risks, and regulatory hurdles. Share repurchases remained a key capital management tool, and the lending pipeline expansion offers a foundation for future growth, though asset quality and margin improvement will require continued discipline and favorable economic conditions.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript
